CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early: three shared–private architectures and an adversarial objective for criterion-invariance in multi-criteria CWS. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (datasets, baselines, F-score results) are informative; however, the notion of “criterion-invariant” features and the adversarial/entropy objective would benefit from a more formal definition or clearer motivation rather than relying primarily on empirical description. Writing quality is adequate overall, with only minor ambiguities in the explanation of failure modes (e.g., how shared parameters introduce noise) that could be clarified. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states the multi-criteria adversarial CWS contributions early, which aligns with practical readers' needs. Notation and model components (shared/private Bi-LSTMs, CRF, discriminator, adversarial objective) are described consistently and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples appear informative for comparing the three architectures and results across eight SIGHAN corpora, though writing is only "adequate" and some sentences could be tightened to reduce minor ambiguity. Overall clarity is good with only minor presentation issues to address.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early: three shared–private architectures and an adversarial objective for criterion-invariance in multi-criteria CWS. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (datasets, baselines, F-score results) are informative; however, the notion of “criterion-invariant” features and the adversarial/entropy objective would benefit from a more formal definition or clearer motivation rather than relying primarily on empirical description. Writing quality is adequate overall, with only minor ambiguities in the explanation of failure modes (e.g., how shared parameters introduce noise) that could be clarified.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states the multi-criteria adversarial CWS contributions early, which aligns with practical readers' needs. Notation and model components (shared/private Bi-LSTMs, CRF, discriminator, adversarial objective) are described consistently and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples appear informative for comparing the three architectures and results across eight SIGHAN corpora, though writing is only "adequate" and some sentences could be tightened to reduce minor ambiguity. Overall clarity is good with only minor presentation issues to address.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates on eight standard SIGHAN corpora using F-score and compares against reasonable neural baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM) under the same data and metrics, so comparisons are largely fair. However, from the provided summary it appears important competing baselines (e.g., other multi-criteria or adversarial multi-task CWS approaches and stronger state-of-the-art systems) are not reported, and it is unclear whether thorough ablations isolate the effect of each component (shared vs. private layers, adversarial loss vs. entropy maximization). The authors acknowledge when gains are marginal and discuss failure modes, but the experimental comparisons stop short of being fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper evaluates across eight standard SIGHAN corpora and compares to sensible baselines (Bi-LSTM and stacked Bi-LSTM), using the same data and F-score metric, which supports fair multi-corpus assessment. However, from the provided summary it appears to omit comparison to other strong/multi-criteria or recent state-of-the-art CWS models (e.g., other multi-task or transfer approaches), and improvements from adversarial training are reported as small/insignificant in places. Overall the comparisons are solid and reproducible but miss a few important competing methods that would make the evaluation fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on eight standard SIGHAN corpora using F-score and compares against reasonable neural baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM) under the same data and metrics, so comparisons are largely fair. However, from the provided summary it appears important competing baselines (e.g., other multi-criteria or adversarial multi-task CWS approaches and stronger state-of-the-art systems) are not reported, and it is unclear whether thorough ablations isolate the effect of each component (shared vs. private layers, adversarial loss vs. entropy maximization). The authors acknowledge when gains are marginal and discuss failure modes, but the experimental comparisons stop short of being fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates across eight standard SIGHAN corpora and compares to sensible baselines (Bi-LSTM and stacked Bi-LSTM), using the same data and F-score metric, which supports fair multi-corpus assessment. However, from the provided summary it appears to omit comparison to other strong/multi-criteria or recent state-of-the-art CWS models (e.g., other multi-task or transfer approaches), and improvements from adversarial training are reported as small/insignificant in places. Overall the comparisons are solid and reproducible but miss a few important competing methods that would make the evaluation fully comprehensive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies known constructs—shared/private Bi-LSTM layers and adversarial training via a criterion discriminator with entropy maximization—to Chinese word segmentation across heterogeneous criteria. While combining these components and proposing three architectural variants (parallel, stacked, skip-layer) is a useful engineering contribution, it is largely an incremental application of established multi-task and adversarial-domain techniques rather than a fundamentally new method. From a theory-oriented perspective the work lacks new formalization or guarantees about what ‘criterion-invariant’ features mean, so novelty is moderate rather than high. | B(3): The paper applies established ideas—shared/private multi-task architectures and adversarial domain-invariant feature learning—to Chinese word segmentation across heterogeneous corpora, and proposes three architectural variants (parallel, stacked, skip-layer). While the specific combination and application to multi-criteria CWS is useful and practically oriented, the core techniques are non-novel extensions of prior shared-private and adversarial domain adaptation methods rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. An expert would find the empirical exploration across eight SIGHAN corpora informative but not surprising.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies known constructs—shared/private Bi-LSTM layers and adversarial training via a criterion discriminator with entropy maximization—to Chinese word segmentation across heterogeneous criteria. While combining these components and proposing three architectural variants (parallel, stacked, skip-layer) is a useful engineering contribution, it is largely an incremental application of established multi-task and adversarial-domain techniques rather than a fundamentally new method. From a theory-oriented perspective the work lacks new formalization or guarantees about what ‘criterion-invariant’ features mean, so novelty is moderate rather than high.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies established ideas—shared/private multi-task architectures and adversarial domain-invariant feature learning—to Chinese word segmentation across heterogeneous corpora, and proposes three architectural variants (parallel, stacked, skip-layer). While the specific combination and application to multi-criteria CWS is useful and practically oriented, the core techniques are non-novel extensions of prior shared-private and adversarial domain adaptation methods rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. An expert would find the empirical exploration across eight SIGHAN corpora informative but not surprising.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem—leveraging heterogeneous segmentation criteria for Chinese word segmentation—and provides a well-engineered multi-criteria BiLSTM-CRF architecture that shows empirical gains across eight SIGHAN corpora. However, from a theory-purist perspective its impact is limited: there is no formalization or guarantees for what 'criterion-invariant' features mean or why the adversarial objective should succeed, ablations isolating shared vs. private components and the adversarial vs. entropy objectives appear incomplete, and reported adversarial improvements are small and sometimes detrimental on large datasets (MSRA, AS). Overall this is a useful empirical contribution likely to be built upon in the CWS community but it does not convincingly change broader theoretical understanding or open major new directions. | B(3): The paper tackles a practical and meaningful problem—learning from heterogeneous Chinese segmentation criteria—by proposing shared/private Bi-LSTM+CRF architectures with adversarial training and evaluating across eight SIGHAN corpora, which is directly useful for practitioners working with multi-corpus or transfer CWS. The approach is likely to influence how researchers design multi-criteria and multitask segmentation systems because the architectures are practical and evaluated on many real-world datasets, but reported adversarial gains are small and sometimes hurt large datasets (MSRA, AS), limiting immediate broad adoption. Overall, the contribution is a useful, empirically grounded engineering advance that opens modest new directions for multi-criteria learning rather than a transformative shift.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—leveraging heterogeneous segmentation criteria for Chinese word segmentation—and provides a well-engineered multi-criteria BiLSTM-CRF architecture that shows empirical gains across eight SIGHAN corpora. However, from a theory-purist perspective its impact is limited: there is no formalization or guarantees for what 'criterion-invariant' features mean or why the adversarial objective should succeed, ablations isolating shared vs. private components and the adversarial vs. entropy objectives appear incomplete, and reported adversarial improvements are small and sometimes detrimental on large datasets (MSRA, AS). Overall this is a useful empirical contribution likely to be built upon in the CWS community but it does not convincingly change broader theoretical understanding or open major new directions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a practical and meaningful problem—learning from heterogeneous Chinese segmentation criteria—by proposing shared/private Bi-LSTM+CRF architectures with adversarial training and evaluating across eight SIGHAN corpora, which is directly useful for practitioners working with multi-corpus or transfer CWS. The approach is likely to influence how researchers design multi-criteria and multitask segmentation systems because the architectures are practical and evaluated on many real-world datasets, but reported adversarial gains are small and sometimes hurt large datasets (MSRA, AS), limiting immediate broad adoption. Overall, the contribution is a useful, empirically grounded engineering advance that opens modest new directions for multi-criteria learning rather than a transformative shift.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong fit for the venue: it addresses an important NLP task (Chinese word segmentation), uses standard datasets and metrics, and presents practically useful multi-criteria/adversarial architectures with solid empirical evaluation. I award a 4 because, while the empirical contribution and relevance are clear, the paper falls short on theoretical formalization of the adversarial objective and lacks fully controlled ablations isolating component effects, preventing it from reaching the highest score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP task (Chinese word segmentation) with a multi-criteria, adversarial shared/private architecture and evaluates on the standard SIGHAN corpora using Bi-LSTM+CRF baselines and F-score — all of which match expectations for a top NLP venue. It is clearly targeted to the venue’s audience and uses accepted datasets and evaluation protocols. However, from a Theory Purist perspective the work leans heavily on empirical gains without formalization of what ‘criterion-invariant’ features mean, lacks theoretical guarantees for the adversarial objective, and does not present fully controlled ablations isolating each component’s contribution, which are minor scope concerns for the highest-tier conferences.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP venue: it tackles a core sequence-labeling problem (Chinese word segmentation) with a practical multi-criteria/multi-domain learning contribution (three shared-private architectures plus adversarial training) that will interest the conference audience. The evaluation uses community-standard datasets (eight SIGHAN corpora), sensible baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM), and standard metrics (F-score), with hyperparameters reported—matching the empirical rigor I expect. Positioning and related-work coverage appear adequate, and the approach directly targets transfer/multi-criteria modeling challenges important to both researchers and practitioners.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is broad and the ablation set-up is reasonable, but they also both flag important weaknesses: limited mechanistic/theoretical analysis, insufficient diagnosis of failure modes where adversarial training hurts, and unclear reproducibility (no code release). Given the solid empirical coverage but these substantive gaps that weaken causal claims and practical reliability, a middle score of 3 best reflects the paper’s strengths and the need for deeper analysis and clearer reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The evaluation is reasonably comprehensive in scale (eight SIGHAN corpora, multiple shared-private architectures, and baseline Bi-LSTM/stacked Bi-LSTM comparisons with hyperparameters reported), but it lacks the mechanistic and theoretical analysis I expect: there is no formal characterization or guarantees for the adversarial objective and only limited diagnosis of when shared parameters hurt (not a thorough failure-mode analysis). Crucially, the summary does not indicate fully controlled ablations isolating each component (shared vs private layers, adversarial loss vs entropy maximization) nor detailed probes of learned features, which weakens causal claims about why the approach works. Reproducibility is also unclear (code not mentioned), so while empirical breadth is good the paper is missing key analyses required by a theory-focused reviewer.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broadly comprehensive: eight SIGHAN corpora and comparisons to Bi-LSTM and stacked Bi-LSTM baselines across three proposed shared-private architectures give strong empirical coverage for the multi-criteria claim. Appropriate ablations are present (three architectures, with/without adversarial training) and hyperparameters are reported, but reported adversarial gains are small and effect heterogeneity (e.g., degradation on large corpora like MSRA/AS) suggests more analysis (statistical significance, per-dataset breakdowns, and error analysis) would strengthen the case. Related work is described adequately and limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility is weakened by no mention of released code and limited exploration of why adversarial training sometimes hurts performance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the method is plausible and evaluated across multiple standard corpora with reasonable baselines, and there are no obvious mathematical errors. However, they also agree the paper lacks theoretical guarantees, thorough ablations, statistical significance analysis, and code release, and reported adversarial gains are small with some negative transfer — weaknesses that limit confidence in the claimed mechanisms. Given these strengths and important empirical rigor gaps, a middle score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a reasonable empirical framework (shared/private Bi-LSTM architectures plus adversarial discriminator) evaluated on eight standard CWS corpora, and reports F-score improvements over Bi-LSTM baselines. However, there is no theoretical formalization or guarantees about what 'criterion-invariant' features mean or about convergence of the adversarial objective, and the description (as summarized) lacks controlled ablations isolating the effects of shared vs private layers and of the adversarial/entropy terms — making it hard to attribute gains mechanistically. Experimental reporting is broader than typical but the authors themselves note only small adversarial gains and potential negative transfer on large datasets, which underscores the need for deeper diagnostics; thus the work is competent but missing key theoretical and ablation evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (adversarial multi-criteria learning with shared/private Bi-LSTM layers improves multi-corpus CWS) and supports it with experiments across eight standard corpora and reasonable baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM) showing aggregate and per-dataset gains. The adversarial objective and architecture designs are plausible and implemented in a standard Bi-LSTM+CRF framework, and hyperparameters are reported, but no code release and no mention of statistical significance tests weaken reproducibility and confidence in small reported gains from adversarial training. There are some acknowledged limitations (cross-criterion bias hurting very large datasets) and no obvious mathematical errors, so the work is technically sound though empirical rigor could be stronger (more baselines, ablations, and significance analysis).

main_claims: Propose adversarial multi-criteria learning framework for Chinese word segmentation that integrates shared knowledge from multiple heterogeneous segmentation criteria.
Design three shared-private architectures (parallel, stacked, skip-layer) to separate criterion-invariant and criterion-specific features for multi-criteria CWS.

Use adversarial training on the shared layer (via a criterion discriminator and entropy maximization) to encourage criterion-invariant features and improve segmentation performance across eight corpora.
_summary.methodology: A Bi-LSTM+CRF neural CWS model with shared and private Bi-LSTM feature layers per criterion; a criterion discriminator is trained adversarially (maximizing entropy of predicted criterion distribution) to force shared features to be criterion-invariant, and training optimizes a combined segmentation and adversarial objective.
datasets: SXU (eight datasets from SIGHAN2005 and SIGHAN2008)
limitations: Adversarial training yields only small additional improvements in some cases (boost not significant).
Shared parameters can bias toward other segmentation criteria and introduce noisy features, potentially hurting performance on large datasets such as MSRA and AS.

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in machine learning theory and long experience analyzing formal properties of sequence models.
traits: penalises papers lacking formal guarantees or theoretical analysis of the proposed objective (e.g., no proof of convergence or formalization of what 'criterion-invariant' means)
requires explicit ablation isolating the effect of each component (shared vs private layers, adversarial loss vs entropy max) and will deduct points if comparisons are incomplete or not controlled
downgrades papers that rely primarily on empirical gains without diagnosing failure modes or providing interpretability (expects feature analyses or probes)
expects clear complexity/efficiency analysis (time/space) and will penalise methods that add nontrivial overhead without justification
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on formal clarity, controlled experiments, and mechanistic understanding; skeptical of empirical claims unless supported by theory or very comprehensive diagnostics.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Industry-affiliated NLP scientist specializing in sequence labeling and multilingual/heterogeneous-data systems, with many deployed segmentation/tagging models.
traits: rewards strong empirical evidence across many real-world corpora and values improvements in aggregate and per-dataset performance (will upscore thorough multi-corpus gains)

favours practical modeling innovations that enable transfer or multitask learning across heterogeneous annotation schemes (will reward multi-criteria frameworks and architectures that are easy to adapt)
values comprehensive baselines and reproducibility: positively scores papers that compare to many prior methods and provide implementation details/hyperparameters
tolerant of limited theory if empirical evaluation demonstrates robustness, scalability, and practical gains
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritizes empirical rigor, real-world applicability, and clear demonstrations that the method improves performance across diverse datasets and baselines.
